So entanglement alone is not sufficient for quantum computational advantage. 2) DQC1 (“one clean qubit”) model: shows certain tasks (estimating normalized trace of a unitary) believed hard classically, while entanglement across many qubits can be small; other correlations like quantum discord may be relevant. But it’s still debated what resources are essential. 3) Measurement-based quantum computation: requires highly entangled resource states (cluster states). There the entanglement structure is necessary, but again not every entangled state is a universal resource. So entanglement is an important resource, but the statement “entanglement causes speedup” is too coarse. One needs a resource theory matched to the computational model (e.g., magic states / non-stabilizerness for fault-tolerant computation).
You’ve stayed mostly in qubit land. Give a continuous-variable entanglement example with EPR-like states, but keep it formal.

In continuous-variable (CV) systems (e.g., modes of the electromagnetic field), a canonical entangled state is the two-mode squeezed vacuum: |ψ(r)⟩ = exp[r (a† b† − a b)] |0⟩_a |0⟩_b, where r ≥ 0 is the squeezing parameter, and a,b are annihilation operators for the two modes. This is a pure Gaussian entangled state. In terms of quadratures x = (a + a†)/√2 and p = (a − a†)/(i√2), the state exhibits reduced variance in EPR-like combinations: Var(x_a − x_b) = (1/2) e^{−2r}, Var(p_a + p_b) = (1/2) e^{−2r}, while individual quadrature variances increase: Var(x_a) = Var(p_a) = (1/2) cosh(2r). Entanglement can be quantified via the symplectic eigenvalues of the partially transposed covariance matrix (Simon criterion). For two-mode Gaussian states, PPT is necessary and sufficient for separability. For the two-mode squeezed vacuum, partial transpose flips sign of one momentum quadrature, yielding a smallest symplectic eigenvalue ν̃ = (1/2) e^{−2r} < 1/2 for any r>0, implying entanglement. Logarithmic negativity is E_N = max(0, −log(2 ν̃)) = 2r (with natural log; in log base 2 it’s 2r/ln 2). This keeps it formal: state defined by unitary squeezing operator, correlations in quadrature variances, entanglement certified by PPT on covariance matrices.
Now tell me what “entanglement sudden death” is and give a simple model where it happens.

Entanglement sudden death is the phenomenon where entanglement of a bipartite system drops to zero in finite time under certain decoherence processes, rather than decaying asymptotically. A simple model: two qubits initially in an entangled mixed state, each undergoing amplitude damping (energy relaxation) with rate γ. The amplitude damping channel for one qubit has Kraus operators: E0 = |0⟩⟨0| + √(1−p) |1⟩⟨1|, E1 = √p |0⟩⟨1|, where p = 1 − e^{−γ t}. Consider an initial “X-state” like ρ(0) = α |11⟩⟨11| + (1−α) |00⟩⟨00| + β (|00⟩⟨11| + |11⟩⟨00|), with parameters chosen so it’s physical and initially entangled. Under local amplitude damping, the off-diagonal coherence term decays and population leaks from |11⟩ into |10⟩,|01⟩,|00⟩. For certain α,β, the concurrence becomes zero at a finite p (finite time), because the decaying coherence can no longer overcome the mixedness introduced by damping. One can write the concurrence for general X-states explicitly: C = 2 max(0, |ρ_{14}| − √(ρ_{22} ρ_{33}), |ρ_{23}| − √(ρ_{11} ρ_{44})). In this model, |ρ_{14}| decays roughly like (1−p) and √(ρ_{22} ρ_{33}) grows due to intermediate populations, causing the expression to cross zero at finite time. So sudden death is not mysterious; it’s a feature of how an entanglement measure depends nonlinearly on density matrix elements under noise.
Now, I want a crisp statement of what entanglement is “in the wavefunction.” If the global state is pure, and reduced states mixed, that’s it? Or is there more?

For pure bipartite states, “entanglement in the wavefunction” is exactly the nontrivial Schmidt decomposition: more than one nonzero Schmidt coefficient. Equivalent characterizations: - |Ψ⟩ is entangled ⇔ Schmidt rank r > 1. - ⇔ reduced density matrix ρ_A is mixed: Tr(ρ_A^2) < 1. - ⇔ entanglement entropy S(ρ_A) > 0. - ⇔ cannot be generated from a product state by local unitaries only. So for pure bipartite states, yes: “global pure, local mixed” is an exact signature. For multipartite systems, it’s richer: there are inequivalent classes of entanglement (e.g., GHZ vs W for three qubits), and reduced mixedness doesn’t fully characterize it.
This has been rigorous enough. Now I want you to anticipate common misunderstandings and correct them with minimal rhetoric, maximal precision.

Common misunderstandings and precise corrections: 1) “Measurement on A physically changes B instantly.” Correction: Local measurement on A changes the conditional state assigned to B given knowledge of the outcome, but the unconditional reduced state ρ_B is invariant under local CPTP maps on A. Therefore B’s local outcome statistics are unchanged without classical communication. 2) “Entanglement is just strong correlation.” Correction: Classical correlations exist in separable states and can be maximal in one basis. Entanglement is non-separability of ρ_AB and manifests as correlations across incompatible observables; it enables tasks (teleportation, distillation) impossible with separable states under LOCC. 3) “Bell violation means faster-than-light signaling.” Correction: Bell violation shows incompatibility with local hidden-variable factorization, not signaling. No-signaling constraints remain satisfied in quantum mechanics. 4) “If two systems are entangled, each has a definite state but unknown to us.” Correction: In entangled pure states, subsystems generally have no pure state at all; their reduced states are mixed, and there is no underlying ensemble of local pure states consistent with all measurement statistics unless you allow nonlocal hidden variables or drop assumptions. 5) “Entanglement is a property of particles.” Correction: Entanglement is a property of the quantum state relative to a chosen tensor-product decomposition (subsystems). In QFT, subsystems are often defined by regions/modes, not particles. 6) “Any entangled state violates a Bell inequality.” Correction: Some entangled mixed states admit LHV models for all projective measurements (e.g., certain Werner states), so they do not violate standard Bell inequalities despite being entangled. 7) “Collapse is a real physical wave traveling.” Correction: Collapse is either an effective update rule (conditioning) or, in interpretations with physical collapse, a nonunitary modification; but either way standard quantum predictions obey no-signaling. The “traveling wave” picture is not part of the formalism.
Last thing: summarize entanglement in one paragraph with no metaphors and no pop-sci phrasing.

Entanglement is the non-separability of a composite quantum state: a bipartite state ρ_AB is entangled if it cannot be written as a convex mixture of product states ∑_k p_k ρ_A^k ⊗ ρ_B^k, and a pure state |Ψ⟩ is entangled if it has Schmidt rank greater than one. Entanglement implies that measurement outcomes on the subsystems exhibit correlations that, for appropriate measurements, cannot be reproduced by any locally causal hidden-variable model (Bell nonlocality), while still obeying no-signaling because local operations do not change the distant reduced state. For pure bipartite states, the entanglement is completely characterized by the Schmidt coefficients and quantified by the von Neumann entropy of a reduced state, which equals the asymptotic rate of interconversion with maximally entangled pairs under LOCC.
